Example 1: Remembering the last time you visited a place
Example 2: Remembering the last time you met with a client
Examples from DS: Remembering a mistake you made on a previous project

Example 2: Recalling information about a customer or client
Examples from DS: Recalling a specific formula or algorithm

Explanation: Remembering how to perform learned tasks or skills
Example 1: Remembering how to drive a car or operate a machine
Example 2: Remembering how to initiate and maintain a conversation at a networking event
Examples from DS: Remembering how to type the correct syntax for a function in a familiar coding language

Explanation: Remembering to do what you had planned to do
Example 1: Remembering to pick up a package after work
Example 2: Remembering to call a client after lunch
Examples from DS: Remembering to fix a bug in the code a day later

Explanation: Holding multiple pieces of information in your mind at once
Example 1: Remembering the architect's plan you just reviewed when renovating a room
Example 2: Remembering the email address you were just told as you type it out
Examples from DS: Remembering the name of the function you just defined a few moments ago

Explanation: Controlling behaviours or thoughts to focus on the task at hand
Example 1: Focusing on the task rather than the noise from the construction site outside
Example 2: Calmly listening to a customer's grievances and resisting the urge to argue back
Examples from DS: Focusing on the current bug even though you have thought of a potential solution for a different one

Explanation: Switching between tasks or adapting to changing circumstances
Example 1: Modifying your usual work process to deal with a specific customer request
Example 2: Sending an email whilst following the discussion in a business meeting
Examples from DS: Adjusting to different syntax after changing between coding languages

Explanation: Imagining possible future scenarios that might result from different actions
Example 1: Imagining what would happen if you removed the bowl from the bottom of the pile of dirty dishes
Example 2: Imagining what would happen if you told a piece of information about a colleague to the manager
Examples from DS: Imagining what would happen if you added a new variable to your machine learning model

Explanation: Mapping out a strategy or a sequence of actions to achieve a goal
Example 1: Deciding that you need to measure and mark the correct location on the wall before drilling
Example 2: Deciding to first greet the host, then approach key individuals, before finally exchanging contact information at a networking event
Examples from DS: Deciding to first clean the dataset, then perform exploratory analysis, before finally training the machine learning model

Explanation: Thinking about or assessing your own thoughts, ability or performance
Example 1: Deciding whether that piece of furniture is too heavy to lift on your own
Example 2: Deciding whether you need notes to deliver a presentation
Examples from DS: Deciding if you have enough understanding of the topic before accepting a project

Explanation: Using prior experience to notice relevant details about people, objects or data
Example 1: Recognising that an object is fragile based on the material it's made from
Example 2: Recognising that a colleague is looking confused during a presentation
Examples from DS: Recognising that a document is formatted incorrectly

Explanation: Recognising the appropriate roles that objects, people, or information can play in achieving a goal
Example 1: Estimating whether a surface is the right size and strength to support an object
Example 2: Recognising that a doctor can diagnose your illness, but a lawyer cannot
Examples from DS: Realising that you can use either a calculator or an excel sheet to do a sum, but only the excel sheet can also contain a database

Explanation: Reasoning about size, space and distance and how you should move from one location to another
Example 1: Deciding whether your luggage will fit in the space that remains in the boot of your car
Example 2: Finding your way from one room to another on the opposite side of a large office building
Examples from DS: Moving files from your local drive to a specific directory in cloud storage

Explanation: Realising that people and objects continue to exist and have impact even if you cannot see them
Example 1: Understanding that while you may have painted over the crack, the house may still be subsiding
Example 2: Understanding that even if someone who is upset leaves the room, they still exist and their emotional state can continue to influence the situation
Examples from DS: Understanding that the data not currently displayed on the screen still exists in the database and will influence the summary statistics

Explanation: Understanding that an outcome is the result of a previous action or event
Example 1: Realising that the change in budget resulted in lower quality materials
Example 2: Realising that a client’s change of heart might be a consequence of the new information they received
Examples from DS: Realising that it was the last change to the code that caused the programme to crash

Explanation: Reasoning about the goals, beliefs and desires of others
Example 1: Being able to understand what a customer is implying, even if they do not say it outright
Example 2: Realising that a client arrived late to a meeting because nobody told them the time had been changed
Examples from DS: Realising that a junior colleague is constantly asking you questions because they are eager to learn

Explanation: Considering and connecting with the feelings and emotions of others
Example 1: Being aware that news of lay-offs might make employees worried or upset
Example 2: Recognising when the client just needs someone to listen and care
Examples from DS: Recognising when a colleague is becoming frustrated and needs to vent

Explanation: Using language to communicate information between you and another person
Example 1: Explaining complex regulations to a colleague in a way that will make sense to them
Example 2: Reading and understanding the latest report from your company's head office
Examples from DS: Explaining to an important client why their proposed solution is not going to work
